title: Linguine all'astice
subtitle: Linguine with king prawns in a tomato and bisque-style sauce
excerpt: Linguine with large prawns in a rich tomato sauce flavoured with prawn shells, white wine, and a little cream. The southern Italian celebration pasta adapted for king prawns, the technique is the same as the lobster original, the accessibility is much greater.
sourceNotes: Based on the Italian linguine all'astice (lobster linguine) tradition from the Italian coastline, documented in Giorgio Locatelli, 'Made in Italy' (2006). Lobster is the traditional and canonical ingredient; shell-on king prawns are used here as a practical substitute that allows the prawn shell sauce-making technique to be retained.

content.text: The technique of linguine all'astice, which involves sautéing the shells before making a rapid bisque-style sauce, applies equally well to large shell-on king prawns. The shells are briefly toasted in olive oil to extract their flavour and colour, deglazed with wine, simmered in tomatoes and stock, and then strained out. The prawns cook in this concentrated sauce for 3 minutes at the end. The result is a deeply flavoured tomato sauce that tastes unmistakably of shellfish without any artificial enhancement.

Use shell-on king prawns and peel them yourself, the shells are where the flavour is. If only pre-peeled prawns are available, the sauce will lack depth and the recipe becomes a simpler gamberi preparation.

items.name: Raw king prawns
items.prepNote: shell-on; peel and reserve the shells

content.text: Peel {{raw-king-prawns}} of king prawns and set the prawns aside. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a high heat. Add the shells and cook for 3 minutes, pressing them with a spoon, until they turn pink and the oil is deep orange. Add {{garlic}} sliced garlic, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, and {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine. Let it bubble for 2 minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes. Simmer for 10 minutes. Strain the sauce through a fine sieve, pressing hard on the shells to extract all the liquid. Discard the shells. Return the sauce to the pan.
Cook the prawns and toss with pasta

Cook {{linguine}} of linguine in well-salted boiling water until al dente. While the pasta cooks, bring the prawn sauce to a simmer and add the peeled prawns. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until just pink. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Reserve a mug of pasta water, drain the pasta, and add to the sauce. Toss over a medium heat, adding pasta water as needed. Scatter {{parsley}} of chopped parsley and serve.

items.fix: The shells need 3 full minutes at high heat. The oil should turn deep orange before the wine goes in.
items.cause: Shells not cooked long enough before deglazing

items.fix: Add prawns in the last 2 to 3 minutes only. They should be just pink when the pasta goes in.
items.cause: Added to the sauce too early or cooked too long

content.text: Linguine all'astice is a dish of the Italian seaside summer. It is pricey, festive, and tied to the restaurant tables of Sardinia, Campania, and Sicily. The lobster is halved live or bought straight from fishing boats. Abroad it has become a prestige pasta dish: shell-sautéing applied to a whole halved lobster. This version uses king prawns. It keeps the method and the flavour without the cost or the work of handling a live lobster.
